title: Inverting Op-Amp Amplifier
description: An operational amplifier configured in inverting topology. Output is 180° out of phase with input. Voltage gain = −Rf/R1.
theory: The inverting amplifier is one of the two fundamental op-amp configurations. In this topology, the input signal Vin is applied through the input resistor R1 to the inverting (−) input of the op-amp. The non-inverting (+) input is connected directly to ground. A feedback resistor Rf connects the output back to the inverting input, completing the negative feedback loop. Due to the op-amp's extremely high open-loop gain (typically ~10^5 to 10^6), the differential input voltage is forced to near zero. This 'virtual ground' principle means that the inverting input (−) is held at approximately 0 V even though it is not physically connected to ground. Using Kirchhoff's current law (KCL) at the virtual-ground node: current through R1 = current through Rf, giving Vin/R1 = −Vout/Rf, so Av = Vout/Vin = −Rf/R1. The negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion. The closed-loop input impedance equals R1. The gain-bandwidth product (GBW) is constant: increasing Av reduces bandwidth proportionally. Slew rate of the op-amp limits high-frequency large-signal response.
keyPoints: Virtual ground: inverting input (−) is held at ~0 V due to negative feedback.
Input impedance = R1 (relatively low — a disadvantage vs. non-inverting topology).
Gain-bandwidth product (GBW) is constant: higher gain → lower bandwidth.
For unity gain (buffer-like), set Rf = R1 → Av = −1 (inverting unity gain).
Both resistors R1 and Rf should be in the kΩ–MΩ range to minimize loading.
Slew rate limits the maximum rate of change of output voltage: SR = dV/dt|max.
examTip: Inverting amplifier: Av = -Rf/R1. Negative sign = 180° phase inversion. Virtual ground: IN- ≈ 0V (due to negative feedback and high open-loop gain ~10^5). Input impedance = R1. Output impedance ≈ 0. Bandwidth: GBW = Av × f3dB = constant (gain-bandwidth product). Slew rate limits high-frequency response.
useCases: Audio signal amplification and mixing circuits.
Summing amplifiers (multiple inputs through separate R1 resistors).
Active filter design (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass with reactive components).
PID controllers in analog control systems.
Signal conditioning and level shifting in sensor interfaces.

wires.comment: R1 right lead to IN- virtual-ground node
Virtual-ground node to op-amp IN- port
Feedback node vertical — connecting Rf left end down to IN- node
Rf right lead to Vout line
Vout vertical line from Rf to op-amp output
V- power rail down to GND symbol

junctions.comment: Virtual-ground node: R1 output + Rf left + op-amp IN-
Vout node: op-amp output + Rf right + Vout wire
